schools.summary: Chrysostom: having said he would eat no flesh forever if it made his brother stumble, Paul now shows by deeds something he had slighted — proving by his own example the self-denial he urged, lest any think his words mere boasting; Paul asserting, by a series of questions, that he is an apostle, free, has seen the risen Christ (on the Damascus road, a mark of apostleship), and that the Corinthians are his work in the Lord (the church he founded, the proof and seal of his apostleship) — the establishing of his apostolic standing and rights, the ground for the argument that he had rights (to maintenance, etc.) which he yet forwent for the gospel.

themes: Am I not an apostle? am I not free?
have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord?
are not ye my work in the Lord?
the apostle's credentials and rights

schools.summary: Gill: this said by way of supposition, not concession — for Paul was an apostle to many (the apostle of the Gentiles), but be that as it may, he was certainly an apostle to the Corinthians; Paul declaring that, even if he were not an apostle to others, he certainly was to the Corinthians, for they are the seal of his apostleship in the Lord — the Corinthian church the proof and confirmation of his apostleship (their conversion and existence as a church, wrought through his ministry, the very seal/authenticating mark that he was sent of God, for God blessed his ministry), the converts the evidence of the blessed ministry.

themes: if I be not an apostle unto others, yet... to you
the seal of mine apostleship are ye in the Lord
the church the proof of his apostleship
the converts the authenticating seal

schools.summary: Gill: these words belong to the preceding — that when any called his apostleship in question and examined him, this (his apostleship and its seal, v.1-2) was his answer in defence of himself; Paul stating that his answer to those who examine (question, scrutinize) him is this, namely the proof of his apostleship and the rights it carried — the apostle's defence against his critics (there were those at Corinth, perhaps the false apostles, who questioned his standing, his refusal of maintenance perhaps taken as a mark against him), pointing to his apostleship, his sight of Christ, and the Corinthian church as his vindication, the calm defence by plain proofs.

themes: mine answer to them that do examine me is this
them that do examine me
the apostle's defence against critics
the vindication by his apostleship

schools.summary: Gill: having proved his apostleship, Paul establishes his right to a maintenance as a gospel minister — by 'power to eat and drink' he means not the common right of mankind but the right to be maintained at the church's expense; Paul asserting his right (power) to eat and drink, that is, to be maintained by the churches he served — the first of the apostle's rights (the right of the gospel minister to a livelihood from those he serves, a lawful claim, to be proved at length, vv.7-14), the right Paul possessed yet forwent (v.15, the point of the chapter, the self-denial), followed by the right to a wife (v.5) and to forbear working (v.6).

themes: Have we not power to eat and to drink?
the right to maintenance
the minister's lawful livelihood
the right possessed yet forgone

schools.summary: Gill: 'a sister, a wife' is a Hebraism (a believing wife); the sense the papists put on it (a woman, not a wife, to minister) is rejected — the other apostles, the Lord's brethren, and Cephas (Peter) had believing wives whom they led about and were maintained with; Paul asserting his right to lead about a believing wife and have her maintained, as did the others — the apostles' right to marriage and a wife's support (a lawful right exercised by Peter and others, refuting the later notion of mandatory apostolic/clerical celibacy), which Paul also had though he did not use it (remaining unmarried for the gospel).

debates.question: Does 'lead about a sister, a wife' support apostolic celibacy or marriage?

sides.position: It refers to a believing wife — the apostles, the Lord's brethren, and Peter were married, refuting mandatory apostolic celibacy (against the papist gloss of a mere ministering woman)

themes: power to lead about a sister, a wife
as... other apostles, and... Cephas
the apostles' right to marry
refuting apostolic celibacy

schools.summary: Gill: Paul and Barnabas were long companions and fellow travellers; 'are we alone exempted from those rights common to others?'; Barnes: they had wrought together as tentmakers (Acts 18:3), but this did not mean they had no claim to support; Paul asking whether he and Barnabas alone lacked the right to forbear working (to be supported without labouring at a trade) — the right to be free from manual labour (the apostles, like other gospel ministers, had the right to be maintained and so to give themselves wholly to the ministry), though Paul and Barnabas chose to work (not because they lacked the right but by choice, for the gospel).

themes: I only and Barnabas
power to forbear working
the right to be supported
they chose to labour for the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: the soldier serves at the public charge (the senate having made an act that soldiers should have a stipend); the apostle argues from common practice that gospel ministers, being soldiers of Christ, should be maintained; Paul arguing for the minister's support by three analogies — the soldier (who serves at another's charges), the vinedresser (who eats the fruit of his vineyard), and the shepherd (who drinks the milk of his flock) — the natural justice of the minister's maintenance drawn from common life (as the soldier, farmer, and shepherd are supported by their labour, so the gospel minister, labouring in Christ's warfare, vineyard, and flock, should be supported).

themes: who goeth a warfare... at his own charges?
planteth a vineyard, and eateth... of the fruit
feedeth a flock, and eateth... of the milk
the natural justice of the minister's maintenance

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul asks whether he speaks this on his own authority (merely as a man, from common reasoning) or whether this reasonable thing is supported by the authority of God — the law of Moses saying the same; Paul moving from the argument of common life (the soldier, vineyard, flock, v.7) to the higher authority of the law — the grounding of the minister's right not only in natural justice but in the divine law (what reason and common practice teach, the law of God also confirms, the two agreeing), the appeal to the law strengthening the argument (the right resting not only on reason but on God's word).

themes: say I these things as a man?
saith not the law the same also?
the appeal from reason to the law
grounded in the divine law

schools.summary: Barnes: the citation from Deuteronomy 25:4 — to muzzle means to bind the mouth to prevent eating; the ox treading out the corn (to thresh it) was not to be muzzled but allowed to eat of it; Paul citing the law that forbids muzzling the ox, and asking whether God's care is for oxen merely — the principle that the labourer should share in the fruit of his labour (as the ox eats the corn it treads), the law, though about the ox, given chiefly for a higher purpose (v.10, teaching that those who labour, the minister, should be maintained, the principle applied to the gospel minister who labours and should be supported).

themes: Thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn
Doth God take care for oxen?
the labourer's share in the fruit
the law given chiefly for a higher purpose

schools.summary: Gill: God delivered the law about the ox not merely for the ox's sake, but chiefly for the sake of men — the Jewish writers themselves saying the ox is mentioned to express man; Paul declaring that the law (of the unmuzzled ox) was written altogether (chiefly) for our sakes — that the plowman should plow in hope and the thresher partake of his hope — the higher application (the law's deeper purpose being to teach that the labourer, the minister, should work in the hope of sharing in the fruit, and should indeed partake/be maintained), the deeper purpose of the law (not the ox merely but the principle of the labourer's right to share).

themes: For our sakes, no doubt, this is written
he that ploweth should plow in hope
the labourer working in hope of the fruit
the deeper purpose of the law

schools.summary: Gill: the preachers of the gospel are compared to sowers; the seed they sow is the word of God; if they have sown the choice spiritual seed, it is no great thing to reap temporal support; Paul arguing that, having sown spiritual things (the gospel) among the Corinthians, it is no great thing for the ministers to reap their carnal (temporal) things — the equity of the exchange (the minister gives the greater, spiritual and eternal benefits, and receives the lesser, material support, so the maintenance is a small and just return), the value of the spiritual over the temporal (the things sown far exceeding the things reaped).

themes: if we have sown unto you spiritual things
is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things?
the just exchange
the minister gives the greater

schools.summary: Gill: 'this power' is the right of a maintenance, which others (the true ministers, or even false apostles) justly claimed and enjoyed; if they had this right, Paul had it the more (as their father in the faith); Paul arguing that, if others have the right of maintenance over the Corinthians, he has it the more, yet he has not used this right but suffers all things lest he hinder the gospel — the supreme example (Paul forgoing his clear right rather than place any obstacle before the gospel, lest his receiving support give occasion for the charge of preaching for gain, enduring hardship and self-support that the gospel be not hindered).

themes: if others be partakers of this power... are not we rather?
we have not used this power
suffer all things, lest we should hinder the gospel
the right forgone for the gospel

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul takes great care to show that the receiving was not forbidden — adducing the law expressly given concerning the priests, a closer example than the oxen; Paul citing the example of those who minister about holy things, living of the temple, and those who wait at the altar, sharing with the altar — the further argument for the minister's maintenance (drawn from the priests and Levites under the law, who were supported from the temple offerings and the altar, sharing in the sacrifices), showing that God himself ordained, in the law, that those who serve in sacred things be maintained by them (the divine pattern applied to the gospel minister).

themes: they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple
they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar
the priests' maintenance
God ordained sacred ministers be supported

schools.summary: Gill: it is an order and appointment of the Lord Jesus Christ that his ministering servants, who labour in preaching, should be sufficiently provided with a comfortable livelihood; Paul declaring that, even so, the Lord has ordained that those who preach the gospel should live of the gospel — the crowning argument (the express command of Christ himself, Luke 10:7; Matthew 10:10, that gospel ministers be maintained by those they serve, as the priests by the temple, v.13), the divine ordinance resting the minister's right on the firmest ground (the Lord's own appointment, not merely reason, the law, or the priests' example, but Christ's express command).

themes: the Lord hath ordained
they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel
the crowning argument
Christ's own command for the minister's support

schools.summary: Gill: Paul had used none of these arguments or rights for himself; nor did he write these things that it should be done to him — but it were better to die than to have his glorying (in preaching freely) made void; Paul declaring that he has used none of these rights, nor written to claim them (lest they think he was hinting for support), and that he would rather die than have his glorying made void — the strength of his resolve (having established his full right to maintenance, he yet refuses it, holding his free preaching so dear, a ground of joy and rejoicing, that he would rather die than lose it), the disclaimer of self-interest.

themes: I have used none of these things
neither have I written... that it should be so done unto me
better for me to die, than... my glorying void
the refusal of the right

schools.summary: Gill: Paul's sense is that, though he preached the gospel ever so well or freely, he had nothing to glory of in the mere preaching, since necessity was laid upon him; Paul declaring that, though he preaches the gospel, he has nothing to glory of in that, for necessity is laid upon him, and woe to him if he preach not — the constraint of the divine commission (the bare preaching no ground of boasting, since Paul preaches under a necessity, the call and command of God, with a woe upon him if he fail, doing but what he is bound to do), his ground of glorying elsewhere (v.15, in preaching free of charge, the voluntary self-denial he need not do).

themes: though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of
necessity is laid upon me
woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel
the constraint of the divine commission

schools.summary: Gill: Paul supposes a case — if he did this thing willingly (freely, without reward, in love, going beyond the bare duty), he has a reward; but a dispensation (a stewardship) is committed to him regardless; Paul distinguishing — if he does this willingly he has a reward, but if merely under constraint (doing only what he must) a dispensation of the gospel is yet committed to him — the difference between the willing service that earns a reward (the free, self-denying service beyond the bare duty, Paul's ground of reward, preaching free of charge) and the mere discharge of the committed stewardship (the bound duty, the necessity, v.16).

themes: if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward
if against my will, a dispensation... is committed unto me
the willing vs the constrained service
the willing self-denial Paul's ground of reward

schools.summary: Gill: Paul has no reward to claim as a debt (he is but an unprofitable servant doing his duty); the only reward that remains is this — to make the gospel without charge; Paul declaring that his reward is this — that, preaching the gospel, he may make it without charge (free of cost to the hearers), not abusing his right in the gospel — the reward Paul sought (not money, honour, or even a debt-reward, but the joy of preaching the gospel freely, a self-rewarding service), forgoing his right rather than abusing it (using it in a way that might hinder the gospel or be misread as greed), the high motive (the gospel's free course and a clear conscience).

themes: what is my reward then?
make the gospel of Christ without charge
that I abuse not my power in the gospel
the joy of preaching freely

schools.summary: Gill: as an apostle in the highest office, Paul had none over him and was independent for his maintenance, yet he made himself a servant; Paul declaring that, though free from all men, he made himself servant to all, that he might gain the more — the great principle of his ministry (being free, under no obligation and beholden to none, he voluntarily became the servant of all, accommodating himself, in things indifferent, to win the more to Christ), the freedom used not for self but in self-denying service (the aim to gain/win souls), the principle of accommodation for the gospel.

themes: though I be free from all men
yet have I made myself servant unto all
that I might gain the more
freedom used in self-denying service

schools.summary: Gill: Paul became as a Jew (in religious observances) when, for their sake, he circumcised Timothy, purified himself, shaved his head, observed the sabbath — accommodating himself to win them; Paul declaring that to the Jews he became as a Jew, and to those under the law as under the law, that he might gain them — the accommodation to the Jews (in things indifferent, the ceremonial observances, now abrogated but harmless, conforming to remove needless offense and win them), without compromising the gospel (he would not yield in the essentials, as the circumcision of Titus, Galatians 2:3), the limit that only things indifferent were accommodated.

themes: unto the Jews I became as a Jew
to them that are under the law, as under the law
accommodation in things indifferent
without compromising the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: 'them that are without law' are the Gentiles, without the written law of Moses; Paul accommodated himself to them, as if without law — yet with the careful guard that he was not without law to God; Paul declaring that to those without law (the Gentiles) he became as without law — yet not without law to God, but under the law to Christ — that he might gain them — the accommodation to the Gentiles (not imposing the Mosaic law, living as they did in things indifferent, to win them), with the guard against lawlessness (his 'as without law' only in the abrogated ceremonial law, never without the moral law of God, always under the law of Christ, the law of love).

debates.question: Does 'as without law' mean Paul was lawless or antinomian?

sides.position: No — he accommodated only in the abrogated ceremonial law; he was 'not without law to God, but under the law to Christ' (always bound by God's moral law and Christ's law of love)

themes: to them that are without law, as without law
not without law to God, but under the law to Christ
accommodation to the Gentiles
the guard against lawlessness

schools.summary: Gill: Paul became as weak to the weak Christians (those scrupling meats, etc.), so consulting their peace and edification that, rather than offend them, he complied with them; Paul declaring that to the weak he became as weak, and that he is made all things to all men, that he might by all means save some — the summary of his accommodating ministry (adapting himself, in things indifferent, to all — Jew, Gentile, weak — condescending, becoming all things, to win every sort), the single aim the salvation of souls, the limit that the accommodation is in things indifferent not the gospel (he never compromised the truth, only adapted the manner), the model for the evangelist.

themes: to the weak became I as weak
I am made all things to all men
that I might by all means save some
adapting in things indifferent to win every sort

schools.summary: Gill: Paul did all this not for his own temporal advantage, nor to curry favour, nor for wealth or honour, but for the spread of the gospel; Paul declaring that he does all this for the gospel's sake, that he might be a partaker of it with them — the pure motive of his accommodation (not self-interest but the advancement of the gospel and the sharing in its blessings and reward), the self-forgetting aim (not his own gain but the gospel and the souls), the wish to be a partaker of the gospel with those he won (sharing in its blessings, reward, and joy).

themes: this I do for the gospel's sake
that I might be partaker thereof with you
the pure motive
the gospel's advancement and its blessing

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul, having shown the usefulness of condescension and his own self-denial (rising higher than all toward perfection by declining to receive, descending lower than all), now touches them more sharply, turning to the figure of the games to spur them on; Paul drawing the figure of the race — all run, but one receives the prize, so run that ye may obtain — the call to earnest, wholehearted effort in the Christian life (as the runners strain every nerve for the prize, so believers must run the spiritual race with all earnestness to obtain the prize of eternal life, the incorruptible crown, v.25), unlike the games (where only one wins) all who run earnestly may obtain.

themes: they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize
so run, that ye may obtain
the figure of the race
earnest, wholehearted effort

schools.summary: Barnes: 'striveth for the mastery' means engaged in the athletic contests (wrestling, boxing, running); such were rigorously temperate in training; Paul observing that every competitor is temperate in all things, and that they do it for a corruptible crown (a fading garland of leaves) but believers for an incorruptible (the eternal, unfading reward) — the call to self-discipline (as the athlete subjects himself to strict temperance and training for a fading garland, so the believer should exercise far greater self-discipline, subduing the body, appetites, and flesh, for the eternal crown), the contrast of the corruptible and incorruptible crowns.

themes: every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things
a corruptible crown; but we an incorruptible
the athlete's temperance
far greater self-discipline for the eternal crown

schools.summary: Gill: Paul animates the Corinthians by his own example — he ran with cheerfulness and certainty, looking to Jesus, steadfast in his course; Paul declaring that he runs not as uncertainly (not aimlessly, not in doubt of the goal, but with a clear aim) and fights (boxes) not as one beating the air (in vain blows, empty motions) but with real, effective effort (his blows landing on the enemy, his own flesh, v.27) — his own example of purposeful, earnest, effective effort in the Christian race (animating the Corinthians by his pattern).

themes: I therefore so run, not as uncertainly
so fight I, not as one that beateth the air
purposeful, effective effort
Paul his own example

schools.summary: Gill: the allusion is to boxers, who give black eyes; Paul means not a maceration of the body but a keeping it under, subduing its sinful affections and lusts; Paul declaring that he keeps under his body and brings it into subjection, lest, having preached to others, he himself should be a castaway (disapproved, rejected) — the rigorous self-discipline of the apostle; the debate on 'castaway': the Arminian reading that even Paul might finally fall (the possibility of apostasy real), the Reformed reading as the language of holy diligence and the means by which the elect persevere (Paul's care itself the divinely-appointed safeguard, not a fear of final falling) and a warning against mere profession (the preacher himself unsaved).

debates.question: Does 'lest I myself should be a castaway' teach a true believer may finally fall?

sides.position: Yes — even the apostle might finally fall; the warning shows the real possibility of apostasy

It is the language of holy diligence and the means by which the elect persevere (Paul's care the divinely-appointed safeguard, the elect not finally perishing), and a warning against mere profession (the preacher himself unsaved)

themes: I keep under my body, and bring it into subjection
lest... I myself should be a castaway
rigorous self-discipline
the perseverance debate
